_comment: Source: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 (March 2022). Pediatric: Brief Resolved Unexplained Event (BRUE) & Acute Events in Infants. NASEMSO national provisional base.
level_display: All Levels — Interventions Scoped Per Provider Level
condition: Brief Resolved Unexplained Event (BRUE) & Acute Events in Infants
protocol_reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Pediatric: Brief Resolved Unexplained Event (BRUE) & Acute Events in Infants

sections.title: Patient Care Goals
sections.reference: NASEMSO National Model EMS Clinical Guidelines v3.0 — Pediatric: Brief Resolved Unexplained Event (BRUE) & Acute Events in Infants
points: Recognize patient characteristics and symptoms consistent with a BRUE
Promptly identify and intervene for patients who require escalation of care
Choose proper destination for patient transport

sections.title: Patient Presentation
points: Suspected BRUE: An event in an infant less than 1 year old reported by a bystander as sudden, brief (less than 1 minute), unexplained, and completely resolved upon EMS arrival that includes one or more of the following: a. Breathing change (absent, decreased, or irregular)
b. Color change (central cyanosis or pallor)
c. Marked change in muscle tone (hyper- or hypotonia)
d. Altered level of responsiveness (increased, irritability, or decreased)
EXCLUSION CRITERIA:
Any signs or symptoms suggestive of underlying or acute illness or injury present upon EMS evaluation, such as: a. Abnormal vital signs for age (including fever)
Identifiable cause for the event, such as: a. Gastric reflux (spitting up)
History or exam concerning for child abuse or neglect
Color change that involved only redness (e.g., in the face) or isolated hands/feet cyanosis

sections.title: EMR Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMR level (Emergency Medical Responder)
points: Scene safety and PPE; primary survey (ABCDE)
Oxygen via NRM targeting SpO2 94-98% if indicated
Position of comfort; hemorrhage control as needed
CPR/AED if patient becomes pulseless
Epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis if applicable
Naloxone intranasal unit-dose for suspected opioid involvement

sections.title: EMT Interventions
sections.reference: NASEMSO v3.0 — EMT level (Emergency Medical Technician)
points: Blood glucose monitoring; oral glucose if hypoglycemic and alert
12-lead ECG acquisition and transmission
CPAP for respiratory distress
Aspirin for suspected ischemic chest pain
Albuterol SVN for bronchospasm component
IV access maintenance (non-medicated)

sections.title: AEMT Interventions
points: IV/IO access initiation
IV/IO fluid resuscitation as indicated
Waveform capnography
IV analgesics (fentanyl, morphine) per pain management protocol
IV antiemetics (ondansetron) per protocol
IV D50W/D10W for hypoglycemia

sections.title: Paramedic Interventions
points: 12-lead ECG interpretation
Endotracheal intubation for airway compromise
RSI per protocol
Advanced antidysrhythmic or hemodynamic support per specific protocol
All parenteral medications per protocol
Vasopressors for refractory hemodynamic compromise

sections.title: Patient Safety Considerations
points: Regardless of the patient's well appearance, all infants with a history of signs or symptoms suggestive of BRUE should be transported for further evaluation a. By definition, infants who are not completely well-appearing at EMS evaluation do not meet the definition of possible BRUE and should be treated and transported according to local guidelines

Destination considerations a. All patients should be transported to facilities with at least baseline pediatric readiness, i.e., appropriate equipment, resources, and trained staff capable of providing initial emergency care and stabilization to pediatric patients prior to hospital admission or interfacility transfer, if feasible

sections.title: Educational Pearls
points: BRUE is a group of symptoms, not a disease process
If the infant is not completely well upon EMS arrival, this excludes possible BRUE event: a. Treat and transport according to local guidelines
Avoid using 'BRUE', 'ALTE', 'SIDS' (sudden infant death syndrome), or 'near-miss SIDS' terminology with parent/guardian

EMS clinicians play a unique and important role in obtaining an accurate history soon after the event and in observing, documenting, and reporting environmental, scene and social indicators that may point to an alternate diagnosis
High-risk patients with a possible BRUE have worse outcomes and may require emergency department (ED) or inpatient testing, intervention, and/or follow-up
The determination of a BRUE is made only after hospital evaluation, not in the field: a. A few of these infants will die even after hospital evaluation and treatment
All patients should be transported to an ED
Contact medical direction if parent/guardian is refusing medical care and/or transport, especially if any high-risk criteria are present

sections.title: Key Documentation
points: ' Document key aspect of history o The event: . Breathing (apnea or respiratory distress)
Color change (central and/or peripheral)
Change in muscle tone
Level of responsiveness
Event duration
o Pre-event circumstances and history . Event associated with feeding or other activity
History of prematurity
Prior BRUE events (ever or in past 24 hours)

EMR: Scene safety, primary survey, CPR, AED, BVM, bleeding control, oral airway, O2 therapy, epinephrine auto-injector for anaphylaxis, naloxone unit-dose auto-injector
EMT: EMR scope PLUS: NPA, supraglottic airway, CPAP, pulse oximetry, 12-lead ECG acquisition, oral glucose, aspirin, patient own nitroglycerin, albuterol SVN, IM epinephrine, blood glucose monitoring, cervical collar, traction splint
AEMT: EMT scope PLUS: waveform capnography, IO access, IV access initiation, IV/IO fluids, IV medications (dextrose, naloxone, epinephrine, glucagon, analgesics, antiemetics), subcutaneous/intranasal medications, nitroglycerin from stock
Paramedic: AEMT scope PLUS: ET intubation, RSI, cricothyrotomy, needle decompression, manual defibrillation, cardioversion, transcutaneous pacing, 12-lead ECG interpretation, all antidysrhythmics, vasopressors, all medication routes and classes

deterioration_flags: Destination considerations a. All patients should be transported to facilities with at least baseline pediatric readiness, i.e., appropriate equipment, resources, and trained staff capable of providing initial emergency care and stabilization to pediatric patients prior to hospital admission or interfacility transfer, if feasible
scope_notes: EMR scope is the minimum national standard — state/MCA may expand
All interventions subject to local medical director credentialing
Pediatric patients: use weight-based dosing and age-appropriate equipment
Contact medical direction when required by local protocol or for interventions outside standard scope
